# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Kubernetes Deployment YAML for our Python web application. The app runs a Flask API on port 5000. We need 3 replicas for high availability. Keep the configuration simple for quick deployment to our production cluster.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: apps/v1 # version of Kubernetes resource
kind: Deployment         # kind is either "Deployment" or a custom type like PodList, etc... 
metadata:                # Information about the object we're creating. For this app it will contain name and namespace for our application instance  
    labels:             
        app: pythonapp       # This helps us identify which objects to control with Selectors (Pods or ReplicaSets)
    name: py-web           # Name of the Kubernetes resource  - must be unique within a cluster. Max length is metadata.name + '.kube'  
                         # and cannot exceed characters on UID maximum size in cloud provider regions that your region supports (like v1, v2). We have to stick with names as far down we can go!   
spec:                     # These are the fields for Kubernetes resource specification.  They define how it should behave within its cluster - which is controlled by kubelet and not directly modified in any way through users' client libraries/APIs  
     replicas: 3           # Specifies desired number of pods that will be running at one time (Pod anti-affinity). Default value for a Deployment object. You can add more metadata fields here...   
                         # For example, the labels field is used to provide information about which Pod this selector selects when it goes into its current execution phase – like in Pending or ContainerCreating state (like kubectl get pods).  
     selector:             # This ties any traffic going from outside world towards our Kubernetes resources, by creating a subset of available nodes for us to send the selected Pod/Pod Templates. The labels field is used in this as well...  These are tied together with selectors so when one label changes that matches up within these selector rules we get new pods which have been created or can be accessed through kubectl (our client).  
         matchLabels:        # This object contains the set of labels to identify those objects. We will use it as a method for 'which' Kubernetes resources should receive traffic from our application, in this case Python Flask Application...  These selectors are used by kube-proxy which is responsible with routing incoming network packets into pods and handling user requests within the cluster  
           app: pythonapp     # This label matches to objects created on one of these specific labels. It makes sure our Pod or Deployment has been correctly set up...  When a new instance was started, kubelet calls APIs for running Containerized applications with both API Server and Kubernetes control plane (kubectl) - hence 'run' is the name given to its role in setting this.
     template:             # This part contains what gets created when objects get instantiated...  It has more detailed fields than a Pod, but we only use it for specifying creation process of pods  
         metadata:           # The information about object - again many ways could be stored here like labels or annotations. For this app I'm just using standard defined in the beginning part with name and namespace...  We will add more details later as needed..   
             labels:          # Label selectors for pods created from these templates are based on matching selected label set (matchLabels) ...   These must match to create instances of this template object. For example, if your Flask app has a particular way it needs its environment variables or something else then you can use those as labels
               app: pythonapp  # If they 'mathched' with the selector - that means we are creating an instance (Pod) for our application   This label must match to create new instances of this template object. For example, if your Flask App needs a specific way it should set its environment variables or something else then you can use those as labels in Template and Match Labels...
         spec:               # The specification about the pod - how containerized application (Flask API) will run on these Pod instances   This is usually done with Docker image, but we also specify command to execute which Flask app should be started when a Container gets created. For this use case it might just call flask directly...
             containers:      # An array of one or more container objects that make up the application's specification - for example if your Python App needs 2 different versions, you can have multiple instances with its dependencies ...    This spec defines how to start a single Container within these Pod Instances (instanciated by Kubernetes)...  
               - name: pythonapp     # Name of the container in this pod instance. In other words it is identified as one particular part inside that 'pod'.  For our Flask App, we have PythonApp since all parts are tightly bundled together and interacts with each others through flasks' request...  
                 image: pythonapp     # Docker Image from where to start the application - this app will run on top of docker container. In a case scenario you need different version or configurations, then pass it as parameter for your Flask Application in its configuration and use that too! ...   
                   ports:             # An array with objects describing how exposed port (s) should be made available within Pod Instances...   The only required field is the containerPort where we want to expose our app from pod.  Also, there're other optional fields like protocol or hostName which will also influence actual communication between services inside that particular namespace ...
                     - containerPort: '5000'     # Specifies port for this instance in case of running Flask App...   This could be exposed by a user to the outside world (internet).  It might have some value depending on how you set up your service and where it should point, also depends upon network configurations. ...   
                       protocol: TCP       # Indicates protocols for communication within container instance - can either 'TCP' or 'UDP'. Default is not to restrict any specific port by default but this might be an option if there are restrictions on traffic from certain ports...  For example, you could use it while defining your service and say "only accept requests through TCP".
     --- # End of Deployment YAML file for python app.   Repeat above lines to create multiple replicas based upon the requirement provided in this task!
